Chapter 13
Lyapunov-Based Design
Lyapunov's method, introduced originally as an analysis tool, turns out to be a
useful tool in feedback design. Many feedback control techniques are based on the
idea of designing the feedback control in such a way that a Lyapunov function,
or more specifically the derivative of a Lyapunov function, has certain properties
that guarantee boundededness of trajectories and convergence to an equilibrium
point or an equilibrium set. In this chapter, we present four such methods. We
start in Section 13.1 with Lyapunov redesign, a technique that uses a Lyapunov
function of a nominalsystem to design an additional control component to robustify
the design to a class of large uncertainties that satisfy the matching condition;
that is, the uncertain terms enter the state equation at the same point as the
control input. We show how Lyapunov redesign can be used to achieve robust
stabilization, and to introduce nonlinear damping that guarantees boundedness of
trajectories even when no upper hound on the uncertainty is known. The matching
condition assumption can be relaxed via the backstepping technique, introduced in
Section 13.2. Backstepping is a recursive procedure that interlaces the choice of a
Lyapunov function with the design of feedback control. It breaks a design problem
for the full system into a sequence of design problems for lower-order (even scalar)
systems. By exploiting the extra flexibility that exists with lower order and scalar
systems, backstepping can often solve stabilization, tracking, and robust control
problems under conditions less restrictive than those encountered in other methods.
In Section 13.3, we use backstepping and Lyapunov redesign to introduce sliding
mode control, a popular technique for robust control under the matching condition.
In sliding mode control, trajectories are forced to reach a sliding manifold in finite
time, and stay on the manifold for all future time. Using a lower-order model,
the sliding manifold is designed to achieve the desired control objective. Finally,
in Section 13.4, we show the use of Lyapunov's method in the design of adaptive
control by presenting a direct model reference adaptive controller. The analysis

| 578  |     | CHAPTER 13.  |     | LYAPUNOV-BASED DESIGN  |     |
| ---- | --- | ------------ | --- | ---------------------- | --- |
of the adaptive control system makes an interesting application of the invariance
theorems of Section 4.3.
13.1  Lyapunov Redesign

redesign.

582 CHAPTER 13. LYAPUNOV-BASED DESIGN
Hence, with the control (13.1 I), the derivative of V(t,x ) along the trajectories of the
closed-loop system (13.9) is negative definite. Notice that the control laws (13.10)
and (13.11) coincide for single-input systems (p = 1).
The control laws (13.10) and (13.11) are discontinuous functions of the state x.
This discontinuity causes some theoretical as well as practical problems. Theoreti-
cally, we have to change the definition of the control law to avoid division by zero.
We also have t,o examine the question of existence and uniqueness of solutions more
carefully since the feedback functions are not locally Lipschitz in x. Practically, im-
plementation of such discontinuous controllers is characterized by the phenomenon
of chattering where, due to imperfections in switching devices or computational
delays, the control has fast switching fluctuations across the switching ~urface.In~-
stead of trying to work out all these problems, we shall choose the easy and more
practical route of approximating the discontinuous control law by a continuous one.
The development of such approximation is similar for both control laws. There-
fore, we continue with the control (13.10) and leave the parallel development of a
continuous approximation of (13.1 1) to Exercises 13.2 and 13.3.

Thus, in general, the continuous Lyapunov redesign (13.12) does not stabilize the
origin as its discontinuous counterpart (13.10) does.  Nevertheless,  it guarantees
uniform ultimate houndedness of the solutions. The ultimate bound b(r) is a class K
function of r. Therefore, it can be made arbitrarily small by choosing c small enough.
In the limit, as r + 0, we recover the performance of the discontinuous controller.
Notice that there is no analytical reason to require r to be very small.  The only

r as small as feasible because we would like to drive the state of the system to a

Therefore, we shall refer to 6, as the unmatched uncertainty and to as the

stabilization problem of designing a state feedback control to stabilize the origin
(q = 0, ( = 0) in the presence of the uncertainties 6, and 4. We assume that f,

We approach the design via a backstepping approach. We start with the system
i =f (q,0+ 6n(~>F)
<
and view ( as the control input. We seek a stabilizingstate feedback control = 4(q)
with 4(O) = 0, so that the origin of
G = f(% d(7))+ 6v(% 4(?)) (13.45)
is asymptotically stable. We will not dwell on this stabilization problem. We simply
assume that we can find a stabilizing continuously differentiable function +(q). Let
us note, however, that this stabilization problem is the typical stabilization problem
we have studied in the previous two chapters, as well as the first two sections of the
current chapter. The tools of linearization, exact feedback linearization, Lyapunov
redesign, backstepping, or a combination of them could be used toward the design
of 4. To proceed with the design of the sliding mode control, set
= F - 4(7)
It is clear that if z = 0, we have [ = 4(q) and the variable q approaches the origin
asymptotically. In the backstepping approach of the previous section, the control u
is designed to stabilize z and q simultaneously through a Lyapunov function for the
overall system. In sliding mode control, we approach the problem differently. We
design u to bring z to zero in finite time and then maintain the condition r = 0 for
all future time; that is, we make z = 0 a positively invariant set of the closed-loop
system. Toward that end, let us write the i-equation

604 CHAPTER 13. LYAPUNOV-BASED DESIGN
Figure 13.2: A typical phase portrait under sliding mode control.
which shows that trajectories starting off the manifold y = 0 must reach it in finite
time. Thus, a typical motion under sliding mode control consists of a reaching
phase during which trajectories starting off the manifold 2 = 0 move toward it and
reach it in finite time, followed by a sliding phase during which the motion will be
confined to the manifold z = 0 and the dynamics of the system will be represented
by the reduced-order model (13.45). A sketch of this typical motion is shown in
<
Figure 13.2. The manifold z = - #(q) = 0 is called the sliding manifold.
The procedure for designing a sliding mode controller can be summarized by the
following steps:
Design the sliding manifold F = 4(q) to control the motion of the reduced-
order system (13.45).
Estimate ~(q,<a)n d k in (13.50).
+
Take the control u as u = ueq Gil(q,<)v,w here ueq is the equivalent
control given by (13.48) and u is the switching (discontinuous) control given
by (13.52).
This procedure exhibits model order reduction because the main design task is
performed on the reduced-order system (13.45).
An important feature of sliding mode control is its robustness to uncertainties.
To understand this feature, it is important to distinguish between the matched un-
certainty 6( and the unmatched uncertainty 6,. We should also distinguish between
the reaching and sliding phases. During the reaching phase, the tasks of forcing tra-
jectories toward the sliding manifold and maintaining them on the manifold, once
they are there, are achieved by the switching control (13.52). This task is affected
by both the matched and unmatched uncertainties, and robustness is achieved by

13.3. SLIDING MODE CONTROL 605
choosing O(r/,<) 2 p(q,<) + b, where p(q,<) is a measure of the size of uncertainty
as determined by inequality (13.50). Since we do not require p(q,f) to be small,
the switching cont,roller can handle fairly large uncertainties, limited only by prac-
tical constraints on the amplitude of control signals. During the sliding phase, the
niotion of the system, as determined by (13.45), is affected only by the unmatched
uncertainty 6,. Robustness to $ will have to he achieved through the design of
$(q). In general, robust stabilization at the reduced-order level will require restrict-
ing the size or structure of 6,. In the special case when there is only matched
uncertainty, that is, 6, = 0, sliding mode control will guarantee robustness for any
nominal manifold design ( = 4(q) that achieves the control objectives on the sliding
manifold.
The sliding mode controller contains the discontinuous nonlinearity sgn(z;). As
we noted in Section 13.1, discontinuous feedback control raises theoretical and prac-
tical issues. Theoretical issues like existence and uniqueness of solutions and validity
of Lyapunov analysis will have to be examined in a framework that does not require
the state equation to have locally Lipschitz right-hand side functions." There is
also the practical issue of chattering due to imperfections in switching devices and
delays. The sketch of Figure 13.3 shows how delays can cause chattering. It depicts
a trajectory in the region z > 0 heading toward the sliding manifold z = 0. It first
hits the manifold at point a. In ideal sliding mode control, the trajectory should
start sliding on the manifold from point a. In reality, there will he a delay between
the time the sign of z changes and the time the control switches. During this de-
lay period, the trajectory crosses the manifold into the region z < 0. When the
control switches, the trajectory reverses its direction and heads again toward the
manifold. Once again, it crosses the manifold, and repetition of this process creates
the "zig-zag" motion (oscillation) shown in the sketch, which is known as chatter-
ing. Chattering results in low control accuracy, high heat losses in electrical power
circuits, and high wear of moving mechanical parts. It may also excite unmodeled
high-frequency dynamics, which degrades the performance of the system and may
even lead to instability.
One approach to eliminate chattering is to use a continuous approximation of
the discontinuous sliding mode c~ntroller.'~B y using continuous approximation,
we also avoid the theoretical difficulties associated with discontinuous controller^.'^
"To read about differentiale quations with a discontinuous right-hand side, consult 1491, 11761,
[130], and [154].
120ther approaches to eliminate chattering include the use of observer [177] and extending the
dynamics of the system using integrators (1581. It should be noted that the continuous approx-
imation approach can not be used in applications where actuators have to be used in an on-off
operation mode, like thyristors for example (1771.
lawhile we do not pursue rigorous analysis of the discontinuous sliding mode controller, the
reader is encouraged to use simulations to examine the performance of both the discontinuous
controUer and its continuous approximation.

(q('J),t(O)) fin. 0
The theorem shows that the "continuous " sliding mode controller achieves ul-
timate boundedness with an ultimate bound that can be controlled by the design
parameter c. It also gives conditions for global ultimate boundedness. Since the
uncertainty 6c could be nonvanishing, ultimate boundedness is the best we can ex-
pect, in general. If, however, 6c vanishes at the origin, then we may be able to show
asymptotic stability of the origin. This issue is dealt with in Exercise 13.26."
In introducing sliding mode control, we have dealt only with autonomous sys
terns and considered only the stabilization problem. The foregoing discussion can
be easily extended to the nonautonomous regular form
where G,(t, q,E ) is nonsingular for all t 2 0 and all (q,<)i n the domain of interest.
Such extension is pursued in Exercise 13.27. Control objectives other than stabiliza-
tion, like tracking for example, can be handled by the same design procedure. The
only difference comes at the design of the sliding surface E = +(q),w hich should be
designed to meet the control objective for the reduced-order system (13.45). The
forthcoming Example 13.14 will illustrate this point.

Definition 13.1 A vector signal u(t) is said to be perststently exciting if there are

tt6
I u(r)uT(r)d i2 all (13.72)
Although the matrix u(r)uT(r)i s singular for each r, the persistence of exci-
tation condition requires that its integral over an interval of length 6 be uniformly
positive definite. The upper bound in (13.72) is satisfied whenever v(t)i s bounded,
which is the case with our signal w(t).
In view of Lemma 13.4 and Definition 13.1, we can say that the origin of the
closed-loop system (13.69)-(13.70) is exponentially stable if w(t) is persistently
exciting. For all initial states of the system, the state will approach the origin
exponentially fast. However, we cannot say that the origin is globally exponentially
stable. To claim that the origin is globally exponentially stable we must be able to
show that the state of the system satisfies the inequality
Ilx(t)ll 5 Ilx(t~)llkex~[-a-(t t o)] (13.73)
for some positive constants k and o and for all initial states z(t0). This inequality
is not just an exponentially decaying bound; it also requires that the bound depend
linearly on the norm of the initial state. Although we arrive at our conclusions by
studying the linear system x = A(t)z, which ordinarily would give global results,
our linear system representation is valid only as long as A(t) is bounded, which is
the case when w(t) is bounded. The bound on w(t) depends on the initial state
z(to). Therefore, we cannot conclude global exponential stability. What we can say
is that given any constant a > 0, no matter how large it is, all solutions starting in
the hall {llzll < a} will satisfy (13.73). This is done by choosing c large enough to
<
include the hall {11z11 < a] in the set {V c} (notice that V is radially unbounded).
Then, all signals in the closed-loop system can be bounded by constants dependent
on a but independent of the given initial state.
The persistence of excitation condition on w(t)i s not a verifiable condition since
the signal w(t) is generated inside the adaptive control systern and is unknown a
priori; so there is no way for us to check whether or not it is persistently exciting.
Notice, however, that
+ --
w(t) = w,(t) Qe(t), where e(t) 0 as t -+ m
and e(t) and i(t)a re hounded. It is reasonable to conjecture that the persistence of
excitation of w(t) would follow from the persistence of excitation of w,(t), which
is a model signal generated by a Hurwitz transfer function driven by r(t). This is
indeed the case, as we show next with the aid of the following lemma.
Lemma 13.5 Let u(t) and u(t) be piecewise continuous. If u is persistently exciting
and Som IIu(t)ll$ dt < k < M, then u + u is persistently exciting. 0

In our problem, e(t) is a square-integrable function since = -eTDe, with a
positive definite D, and the limit of V as t oo is finite. Hence, the persistence of
excitation of w(1) is implied by the persistence of excitation of w,(t). We can now
summarize our conclusions in the following theorem.
Theorem 13.3 Consider the adaptive control system (13.69)-(13.70) which corre-
sponds to a plant of order n and relative degree 1. Assume that the reference model
transfer function M(s) is strictly positive real. If the input reference r(t) is piece-
wise continuous and bounded, then, for any arbitrary initial state, all signals in the
adaptive control system are bounded (with bounds dependent on the initial state)
and the tracking error
e(1) -0 as t + m
If, in addition, $1) is bounded and w,(t) is persistently exciting, then the origin of
(13.69)-(13.70) is exponentially stable. In particular, for all initial states in a given
ball of arbitrary radius, the state decays to the origin exponentially fast, satisfying
(13.73). 0
A significant question here is: What conditions on r(t) would ensure the persis-
tence of excitation of w,(t)? Since w,(t) is the output of a linear system (with a
Hurwitz transfer function) driven by r(t), this question is addressed via linear anal-
ysis. We shall not pursue this issue here,25 but let us note that the ultimate result
of such investigation is that the input r(t) should be "sufficiently rich to guaran-
tee that w,(t) is persistently exciting. For example, if r(1) is the sum of distinct
siuusoids, then it should have at least n distinct frequencies. The intuition behind
the persistence of excitation condition is that the input should be rich enough to
excite all modes of the system that is being probed.

reference is rich enough to guarantee persistence of excitation. From Theorem 13.3,
we conclude that for any ro  # 0, the origin will be exponentially stable.  What
about ro = 0 ? When ro = 0, the signal w,(t)  is not persistently exciting, and we

A crucial question in studying the stability of any dynamical system is the ro-
bustness of the stability properties of the system to perturbations in the system's
model. We have come across this question at several points, but we examined the
question in some depth in Chapter 5. A fundamental fact that we gather from that
chapter is that an exponentially stable equilibrium point is robust with respect to
both vanishing and nonvanishing perturbations in the sense that the stability prop-

13.4. ADAPTIVE CONTROL 635
erties of the system will not be destroyed by arbitrarily small pexturbations. Of
course, with nonvanishing perturbations we cannot guarantee preservation of the
exponential stability of the origin, but we can guarantee uniform ultimate bounded-
ness. This fact is of fundamental importance when we analyze the adaptive control
system under consideration. According to Theorem 13.3, if the persistence of exci-
tation condition is satisfied, then the origin is exponentially stable. This statement
carries with it a statement on the robustness of the adaptive control system. We
cannot cnsure such robustness in the lack of persistence of e~citation.~~

13.4. ADAPTIVE CONTROL 637
We remarked before that the exponential stability statement of Theorem 13.3
is not global. This bas an important implication when we study robustness of the
adaptive control system. We know from Chapter 5 (or Chapter 6 on input-output
stability) that if the origin of the nominal system is globally exponentially stable,
then for any bounded disturbance, irrespective of its size, the state of the system
will be bounded for all t >_ 0. In the lack of global exponential stability, we can
guarantee boundedness of the state only if the size of the disturbance is limited as
we have just done in the previous example. The next example shows that, if the
size of the disturbance is not limited, a bounded disturbance may drive the state
unbounded.
Example 13.18 Consider the adaptive control system of Example 13.16, and as-
sume there is a disturbance v(t) that affects the system at the plant input; that is,
the plant is described by
+ +
yp = QpYp u v(t)
Take ym(t) E ro. The closed-loop system is described by
For TO # 0, the persistence of excitation condition is satisfied; hence, we can repeat
the analysis of the previous example. We leave that to the reader (Exercise 13.44)
and proceed to investigate the implication of having a large disturbance. Suppose
ro > 0 and v(t) = -uo with vo > TO. The closed-loop system is described by the
second-order autonomous state equation
+ +
& = -e, d(e, ro) - vo
+
4 = -ye,(e, ra)
Let D denote the region of the state plane which lies to the left of the branch of
the curve +
Q=- e, yo (13.74)
+
e, ro
on the left side of e, = -ro. The set D is a positively invariant set. This fact can be
seen by noting that on the curve (13.74), 6, = 0 and 4 is negative. Thus, the vector
field on the curve (13.74) always points into the set D. It can he easily seen that
there are no equilibrium points in D. Moreover, by an application of Bendixson's
criterion,z7 it can he seen that there are no periodic orbits in- D . Thus, for this
second-order, system, the only option left for the state is to escape to infinity. In
fact, since 4 is always negative in D, it is clear that 4(t) -03 as t -; m.
2TSeeT heorem 7.2 and Exercise 7.5.

638 CHAPTER 13. LYAPUNOV-BASED DESIGN
This confirms the point that the robustness of the system to bounded disturbance
cannot be guaranteed if the disturbance is arbit,rarily large. Notice that the size
of the disturbance that drives the state unbounded is related to the size of the
reference ro. This can be given the following interpretation. For the persistence of
excitation condition to hold, we must have ro > 0; the larger the value of ro, the
stronger the exponential stability of the nominal system and the more robust the
system is. The analysis of this example has a ser-iou s implic-at ion in the case ro = 0.
When ro = 0, the nominal system does not have exponential stability at the origin,
although we were able to conclude that e,(t) 0 as t oo. The analysis we
have just perfo-rm ed shows that with a constant disturbance v(t)= -vo, the state
of the system could go unbounded even when vo is arbitrarily small. Thus, the
property e,(t) 0 of the nominal system is not robust because it cannot withstand
arbitrarily small perturbations. Clearly, such an adaptive control system is not
practical. A

A.7 Proof of Theorems 4.1 and 4.3
The main element in the proofs of these two theorems is a contraction mapping
argument, which is used almost identically in the two proofs. To avoid repetition,
we shall state and prove a lemma which captures the needed contraction mapping
argument and then use it to prove the two theorems. The statement of the lemma
appears to he very similar to the statement of Theorem 4.1, but it has an additional
claim that is needed in Theorem 4.3.
Lemma A.2 Consider the system
where y E Rk, z E Rm, the eigenvalues of A have zero real pads, the eigenvalues
of B have negative real parts, and f and g are twice continuously differentiable
functsons which vanish together with their first derivatives at the origin. Then,
there exist 6 > 0 and a continuously differentiable function q(y), defined for all
11~11< 6, such that z = q(y) is a center manifold for (A.7)-(A.8). Moreover, if
11g(y, 0)))5 kJlyJIPf or all llyll 5 r where p > 1 and r > 0, then there is c > 0 such
<
that Ilv(~)ll cll~ll~. 0
Proof: It is more convenient to show the existence of a center manifold when
solutions in the manifold are defined for all t 6 R. A center manifold for (A.7)-
(A.8) may, in general, he only local; that is, a solution in the manifold may be
defined only on an interval [O, 1,) c R. Therefore, the following idea is used in the
proof. We consider a modified equation which is identical to (A.7)-(A.8) in the
neighborhood of the origin, hut has some desired global properties which ensure
that a solution in a center manifold will he defined for all t. We prove the existence
of a center manifold for the modified equation. Since the two equations agree in the
neighborhood of the origin, this proves the existence of a (local) center manifold for
the original equation.

I
662 - APPENDIX A. PROOFS
Let $ : Rk [O, 11 be a smooth (continuously differentiable infinitely many

Lemma A.3  The boundary of  an open invariant set is an invariant set. Hence, it
| is formed of  | trajectories.  |     |     |     | A   |
| ------------- | -------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
ProoE  Let M be an open invariant set and z be a boundary point of M. There
exists a sequence z,  E M which converges to z. Since M is an invariant set, the
solution #(t,z,)  E M for all t  E R. The sequence 4(t, z,)  converges to 4(t, x) for
all t  E R.  Hence, 4(t, x) is an accumulation point of M for all t.  On the other
hand, 4(t, x) @ M since x is a boundary point of M. Therefore, the solution 4(t, x)
belongs to the boundary of M for all t.

A.10 Proof of Theorem 7.1
We prove the Poincar&Bendixson theorem only for positive limit sets. The proof
for negative limit sets is similar. We start by introducing transversals with respect
to a vector field f. Consider the second-order equation
where f : D -+ R2 is locally Lipschitz over a domain D C R2. A transversal with
respect to f is a closed line segment L E D such that no equilibrium points of
(A.20) lie on L and at every point x E L, the vector field f(x) is not parallel to the
direction of L. If L is a segment of a line whose equation is
then L is a transversal off if
If a trajectory of (A.20) meets a transversal L, it must cross L. Moreover, all such
crossings of L are in the same direction. In the next two lemmas, we state the
properties of the transversals which will be used in the proof.
Lemma A.4 If yo is an interior point of a transversal L, then for any c > 0
there is 6 > 0 such that any trajectory passing through the ball Bs(yo) = {z E
R2 1 111- yo11 < 6) at t = 0 must cross L at some time t € (-r, 6). Moreover, if the
trajectory is bounded, then by choosing c small enough the point where the trajectory
crosses L can be made arbitrarily close to yo. A

Lemma A.5 If a trajectory y, followed as t increases, crosses a transversal at three
consecutive points yl, yz, and y3, then yz lies between yl and y3 on L. A
Proof: Consider the two consecutive points yl and yz. Let C be a simple closed
(Jordan) curve made up of the part of the trajectory between yl and yz (the arc
MNP in Figure A.l) and the part of the transversal L between the same points
(the segment PQM in Figure A.l). Let D be the closed bounded region enclosed
by C. We assume that the trajectory of yz enters D; if it leaves, the argument is
similar. By uniqueness of solutions, no trajectory can cross the arc MNP. Since L
is a transversal, trajectories can cross L in only one direction. Hence, trajectories
will enter D along the segment PQM of L. This means the set D is positively
invariant. The trajectory of yz must remain in the interior of D which, for the
case sketched in Figure A.l, implies that any further intersections with L must take
place at a point below y,.
The next lemmais concerned with the intersection of limit sets and transversals.
Lemma A.6 Let y be a positive limit point of a bounded positive semiorbit yt.
Then, the trajectory of y cannot cross a transversal at more than one point. A

A.15 Proof of Theorem 9.4
The proof of this theorem follows closely the proof of Theorem 9.1. We shall only
point out the differences. Notice first that due to the extra assumptions, the Jaco-
bian [dF/dz](t, z, 0,O) has bounded first partial derivatives with respect to z. Also,
due to exponential stability of the origin of the reduced model, given rl < T there
exists p1 > 0 such that for ll<oll < p1 the solution E(t) of (9.8) is defined for all
t 2 to and satisfies IIZ(t)l 5 rl.

Notes and References
The main references used in the preparation of this text are Hirsch and Smale [TO],
Hale [65], and Miller and Michel [I181 for the theory of ordinary differential equa-
tions; Hahn [62], Krasovskii [96], and Rouche, Habets, and Laloy 11371 for stability
theory; and the texts by Vidyasagar [18l](first edition) and Hsu and Meyer [76].
References for the specialized topics of Chapters 4 to 13 are listed under the respec-
tive chapters.
Chapter 1. The tunnel diode circuit and negative resistance oscillator are
taken from Chua, Desoer, and Kuh [33]. The presentation of the mass-spring sys-
tem is hased on Mickens [I171 and Southward [165]. The Hopfield neural network
description is hased on Hopfteld [71] and Michel, Farrel and Porod [114]. The adap-
tive control example is based on Sastry and Bodson (1511. The classical material
on second-order systems can be found in almost any text on nonlinear systems.
Our presentation has followed closely the lucid presentation of Chua, Desoer, and
Kuh [33]. Section 1.2.5 is based on Parker and Chua [132].
Chapter 2. The section on mathematical preliminaries has been patterned af-
ter similar sections in Bertsekas 1211 and Luenberger [106]. For a complete coverage
of topics in this section, the reader may consult any text on mathematical analysis.
We have used Apostol [7]. Other standard texts are Rudin [I401 and Royden (1391.
The material in Sections 2.2-2.4 is standard and will he found in one form or the
other in any graduate text on ordinary differential equations. Section 2.2 comes
very close to the presentation, in Vidyasagar [181], while Sections 2.3 and 2.4 are
based on Hirsch and Smale (701 and Coppel [36]. The comparison principle is hased
on Hale [65], Miller and Michel [I181 and Yoshizawa [189].
Chapter 3. Hahn [62], Krasovskii 1961, and Rouche, Habets, and Laloy [I371 are
authoritative references on Lyapunov stability. The presentation style of Section 3.1
is influenced by Hirsch and Smale [70]. The proof of Theorem 3.1 is taken from
Hirsch and Smale [70, Section 9.31, while that of Theorem 3.3 is hased on Hahn [62,
Section 251, Hale [65], and Miller and Michel [118]. The invariance principle pre-

706 NOTES AND REFERENCES
sentation of Section 3.2 follows the original work by LaSalle [99]. The proof of
Lemma 3.1 is based on Rouche, Hahets, and Laloy [137, Appendix III]. The appli-
cation of the invariance principle to neural networks is standard and can he found
in Hopfield [71]. Our presentation is influenced by Salam 11471. See also Cohen and
Grossberg [35] for a generalization of Example 3.11. The material on linear time-
invariant systems in Section 3.3 is taken from Chen [29]. The proof of Theorem 3.6
is taken from Kailath [86]. The proof of Theorem 3.7 on linearization is guided by
Rouche and Mawhin [138, Sections 1.6 and 1.71, which includes a careful treatment
of the case when the linearization has at least one eigenvalue in the right-half plane
with other eigenvalues on the imaginary axis. The proof of Lemma 3.3 is taken
from Hahn [62, Section 35) (for the local part) and Lin, Sontag, and Wang [I041
(for the global part). The statement and proof of Lemma 3.4 is guided by Hahn [62,
Section 24E] and Sontag [162, Lemma 6.11. The proof of Lemma 3.5 is taken from
Hahn [62, Section 24A]. The proof of Theorem 3.8 combines ideas from Hahn [62,
Section 251 and Rouche, Habets, and Laloy [137, Section 1.61. Section 3.5 is based
on Vidyasagar [181]. The proof of Theorem 3.12 is taken from Krasovskii [96, The
orem 11.1]. The proof of Theorem 3.14 is based on Miller and Michel [118, Section
5.131 and Hahn [62, Section 491, with some insight from Hoppensteadt [72].
Chapter 4. Section 4.1 is based mostly on Carr [28], with help from Gucken-
heimer and Holmes [59]. The proof of Theorems 4.1 and 4.3 is taken from Carr [28,
Chapter 21. The proof of Theorem 4.2 using Lyapunov analysis is much simpler
than the proof given in Carr [28]. We have dropped from the statement of the re-
duction theorem the case when the origin of the reduced system is stable. Dropping
this case is justified by the fact that when we apply the theorem, we almost always
use approximations of the center manifold. The only stability properties of the
origin which can be concluded from an approximate model are asymptotic stability
and instability since they are robust to perturbations. The version of the center
manifold theorem quoted in Guckenheimer and Holmes [59] also drops the case of
a stable origin. Corollary 4.1 deals with a stable origin in a special case. This
corollary was suggested to the author by Miroslav Krstic. The proof of Lemma 4.1
is based on Hahn [62, Section 331. Example 4.11 is taken from Willems [186]. The
trajectory-reversing method is described in Genesio, Tartaglia, and Vicino [55].
Other methods for estimating the region of attraction are described or surveyed in
131, 55, 116, 1271. The proof of Lemma 4.2 is taken from Popov [135, page 2111.
The proof of Theorem 4.5 is based on Sastry and Bodson [151, Theorem 1.5.21.
Chapter 5. The material of Sections 5.1 and 5.2 is based on a vast literature
on robustness analysis in control theory. We can say, however, that the basic ref-
erences are Hahn 162, Section 561 and Krasovskii [96, Sections 19 and 241. Similar
results are given in Coppel 136, Section 111.31 for the case when the nominal sys-
tem is linear, but the derivation is not based on Lyapunov theory; instead, it uses

properties of fundamental matrices. The proof of Theorem 5.1 is guided by Miller
and Michel [118, Theorem 9.141 and Corless and Leitmann [38]. The case of nonva-
uishing perturbations is referred to in Hahn 1621 and Krasovskii [96] as the case of
"persistent disturbance." We have decided not to use this term so that it would not
be confused with the term "persistent excitation," which is common in the adaptive
control literature. The results on nonvanishing perturbations are also related to the
concept of total stability; see Hahu [62, Section 561. The concept of input-to-state
stability was introduced by Sontag 11621, who proved a few basic results; see [163].
Our presentation benefited from a nice exposition in Krstic, Kanellakopolous, and
Kokotovic 1971. The comparison method of Section 5.4 is based on sporadic use
of the comparison lemma in the control literature. Theorem 5.3 on continuity of
solutions on the infinite-time interval is novel in that continuity results are usually
stated on compact time intervals, as we have them in Section 2.3. Inclusion of
an infinite-time version is appropriate in view of the fact that in most engineering
problems we are interested in solutions reaching steady-state. Technically, the the-
orem is a straightforward corollary of the results of Section 5.4. Section 5.6 on the
stability of interconnected systems is based mostly on the tutorial paper Araki [a],
with help from the research monographs Siljak [I561 and Michel and Miller [115].
The neural network example is taken from Michel, Farrel, and Porod [114]. The
treatment of slowly varying systems is based on Desoer and Vidyasagar [43, Section
IV.81, Vidyasagar [181], Kokotovic, Khalil, and O'Reilly [95, Section 5.21, and Hop-
pensteadt [72]. Lemma 5.11 is a specialization of Lemma 2 of Hoppensteadt 1721 to
the case of exponential stahility.
Chapter 6. The treatment of C-stability in Sections 6.1 and 6.2 is based on
Desoer and Vidyasagar 1431 and Vidyasagar [181]. The concept of input-to-output
stability is due to Sontag [162]. Section 6.4 on the &-gain is hased on van der
Schaft [178], with help from the papers by Willems [I851 and Hill and Moylan 167,691
on dissipative systems. The proof of Theorem 6.4 is based on 143, Section 2.61, [180,
Section 3.1.21 and [195, Section 4.31
Chapter 7. The section on second-order systems is hased on Hirsch and
Smale [70, Chapters 10 and 111 and Gnckenheimer and Holmes [59, Section 1.81.
The proof of Theorem 7.1 is based on Hirsch and Smale [70, Chapter 101 and Miller
and Michel [118, Section 7.21. The discussion on the stability of periodic solutions
in Section 7.2 is hased on Hahn [62, Section 811, Miller and Michel [118, Section
6.41, and Hale [65, Section VI.21. The Poincarb map presentation is based on Hirsch
and Smale [70, Chapter 131 and Guckenheimer and Holmes [59, Section 1.51.
Chapter 8. The perturbation method of Section 8.1 is classical and can
be found in many references. A detailed treatment can be found in Kevorkian
and Cole 1891 and Nayfeh [124]. The asymptotic results of Theorems 8.1 and

708 NOTES AND REFERENCES
8.2 are adapted from Hoppensteadt's work on singular perturbations; see Bop-
pensteadt 1731. The presentation of the averaging method in Section 8.3 is based
on Sanders and Verhulst [150], Hale [65, Section V.31, Halanay [63, Section 3.51,
and Gnckenheimer and Holmes 159, Sections 4.1 and 4.21. The vibrating pendulum
example is taken from Tikhonov, Vasileva, and Volosov [174]. The application of
averaging to weakly nonlinear oscillators in Section 8.4 is based on Hale [65, pp.
183-1861, The presentation of general averaging in Section 8.5 is based on Sanders
and Verhulst [150], Hale [65, Section V.31, and Sastry and Bodson [151, Section 4.21.
Chapter 9. The presentation of the singular perturbation method follows very
closely Kokotovic, Khalil, and O'Reilly [95]. The proofs of Theorems 9.1 and 9.4
adapt ideas from Hoppensteadt [72]. W? have not included the construction of
higher-order approximations. For that, the reader may consult Hoppensteadt (731,
Bntuzov, Vasileva, and Fedoryuk [24], or O'Malley[128]. The articles [72], [73], and
[24] appear in Kokotovic and Khalil [94]. Example 9.8 is taken from Tikhonov,
Vasileva, and Volosov [174]. For extensions of this example, see Grasman [58]. The
proof of Theorems 9.2 and 9.3 is based on Saberi and Khalil [144].
Chapter 10. Absolute stability has a long history in the control theory liter-
ature. For a historical perspective, see Hsu and Meyer [76, Sections 5.9 and 9.51.
Our presentation of the circle and Popov criteria is based on Hsu and Meyer [76,
Section 9.5 and Chapter 101, Vidyasagar [181], Siljak [155, Sections 8.6 to 8.9 and
Appendix HI, and Moore and Anderson [121]. A comprehensive coverage of abso-
lute stability can be found in Narendra and Taylor [123]. The proof of Lemma 10.1
is hased on Tao and Ioannou [171] and Wen 11821. The proof of Lemma 10.2 is
based on Anderson [2]; see also Anderson and Vongpanitlerd [5] for an expanded
treatment of the positive real lemma. Section 10.1.3 is hased on Boyd and Yang [22]
and Horisberger and Belanger 1741. The small-gain theorem presentation is based
on Desoer and Vidyasagar [43] and the tutorial article by Teel, Georgiou, Praly,
and Sontag [173]. Example 10.9 is taken from [173]. Section 10.3 on the passiv-
ity approach is based on several references, including [68, 26, 97, 173, 1811. De-
tailed treatment of the describing function method can be found in Atherton [13]
and Hsu and Meyer [76, Chapters 6 and 71. Our presentation in Section 10.4 fol-
lows Mees [Ill, Chapter 51. The proof of Lemma 10.7 is taken from Mees and
Bergen [113]. The proof of Theorem 7.2 is based on Mees and Bergen [I131 and
Bergen and Franks [19]. The presentation of the error analysis has gained a lot from
a lucid presentation in Siljak [155, Appendix GI, from which Example 10.24 is taken.
Chapter 11. The design via linearization approach of Section 11.2 is standard
and can be found in almost any book on nonlinear control. The use of integral
control is also standard, hut the results given here can be traced back to Huang
and Rugh [77] and Isidori and Byrnes [82]. The gain scheduling presentation of Sec-

tion 11.3 is based Lawrence and Rugh [loll and Kaminer, Pascoal, Khargonekar,
and Coleman [SS], with help from Astrom and Wittenmark [ll], Rugb [141], and
Shamma and Athans [153].
Chapter 12. The exact feedback linearization chapter is based mainly on
Isidori [SO]. The lucid introduction of Section 12.1 is based on Spong and Vidyasagar
[166, Chapter 101 and Isidori [SO, Chapter 41. The local stabilization and tracking re-
sults are based on Isidori [80, Chapter 41. The global stabilization material is based
on many references, as the global stabilization problem has caught the attention of
several researchers in recent years. The papers [25, 39, 105, 109, 145, 169, 1751 and
the book [97] will guide the reader to the basic results on global stabilization, not
only for the problem formulation presented here, but also for more general problem
formulations, including problems treated in the backstepping Section 13.2. Exam-
ples 12.14, 12.15, and 12.16 are taken from [25], [169], and [52], respectively.
Chapter 13. The Lyapunov redesign section is guided by Corless and Leit-
mann [38], Barmish, Corlem, and Leitmann [14], and Spong and Vidyasagar [166,
Chapter 101. See also Corless [37l for a survey of the use of Lyapunov redesign
in the control of uncertain nonlinear systems. The robust stabilization design of
Section 13.1.1 is also known as min-max control. The nonlinear damping of Sec-
tion 13.1.2 is h a d on Kmtic, Kanellakopolous, and Kokotovic [97, Section 2.51.
The backstepping section is based mainly on the same reference [97], with help
from Qu [136] and Slotine and Hedrick [160]. The book 1971 contains a compre-
hensive treatment of the backstepping design procedure, including its application
to adaptive control of nonlinear systems. The sliding mode control section is based
on the books by Utkin [I761 and Slotine and Li [161], and the tutorial paper by
DeCarlo, Zak, and Matthews [42]. Analysis of the continuous approximation of
sliding mode controllers is based on [45]. The adaptive control section is based on
Sastry and Bodson [151], Anderson and others [4], Narendra and Annaswamy [122],
and Ioannou and Sun [79]. The proof of Lemma 13.4 is based on Anderson [3] and
Anderson and others [4, Section 2.31. The proof of Lemma 13.5 is based on Sastry
and Bodson [151, Lemma 2.6.61.
Other references, which were consulted occasionally during the preparation of
this text, are included in the bibliography.
